aliases: premixed ginger tea with creamer and sugar

english: premixed ginger tea with creamer and sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for 3-in-1 ginger tea; closest defensible match is instant premixed tea beverage (FOODON_03301099). Ginger-specific and 3-in-1 format not individually coded in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for 3-in-1 ginger tea with creamer and sugar in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for instant 3-in-1 ginger tea beverage. SNOMED CT does not granularly code commercial instant tea mixes.

allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.description: 1 sachet prepared as directed (approximately 200 ml beverage)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 20 g of powder) dissolved in 180–200 ml of hot water, yielding one cup of ginger tea.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Flavoured Ginger Tea — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '3 in 1 flavoured ginger tea (any flavour)', CR ID D09020001. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Confidence 0.95 per HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 3-in-1 instant ginger tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food 'sweetened instant tea beverage' and sucrose-dominant sugar profile (GI ~65 for sucrose per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Serving size 20 g powder; carbohydrate per serving = 10.31 g/100g × 20 g = 2.062 g. GL calculated on 20 g sachet basis: (65 × 2.062 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 1.34.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.46 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, despite very low sodium (4 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this product; the high sugar load (9.46 g/100 g) in liquid form independently elevates blood pressure risk regardless of the low sodium content, and plain unsweetened ginger tea brewed from fresh ginger is the recommended alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.46 g sugar/100 g and only 0.17 g dietary fibre/100 g; liquid sugar is rapidly absorbed, causing acute postprandial blood glucose spikes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this product entirely, as the high free-sugar content (9.46 g/100 g) with negligible fibre (0.17 g/100 g) causes rapid glycaemic excursions; the ADA classifies all sugar-sweetened beverages as unsuitable for T2D management, and unsweetened ginger tea is the appropriate substitute.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (9.46 g/100 g) in a beverage contributes to elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; fat content is negligible (0 g/100 g) and sodium is very low (4 mg/100 g).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this product due to its high sugar content (9.46 g/100 g), which is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased CVD risk per AHA dietary guidelines; the absence of saturated fat and very low sodium are mitigating factors, but the sugar load remains a primary concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar content (9.46 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (0.17 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators, negating any anti-inflammatory benefit from ginger-derived gingerols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this product; the high sugar content (9.46 g/100 g) promotes systemic inflammation and negates the anti-inflammatory gingerol properties of ginger, and brewing fresh unsweetened ginger tea is the recommended alternative to obtain therapeutic benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Flavoured Ginger Tea (Any Flavour) is an ultra-processed instant beverage popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia, sold in individual sachets containing a pre-blended mixture of ginger powder, non-dairy creamer, sugar, and additional flavourings. The product is prepared by dissolving one sachet (approximately 20 g) in 180–200 ml of hot water, yielding a warm, mildly spiced, sweet tea with a characteristic ginger aroma and a creamy mouthfeel from the non-dairy creamer component. The flavour profile is dominated by sweetness (sugar 9.46 g/100 g) with a gentle ginger warmth and light creaminess. Per 100 g of powder, the product provides 41 kcal, 0.02 g protein, 0 g fat, 10.31 g carbohydrate (of which 9.46 g is sugar), 0.17 g dietary fibre, and 4 mg sodium. The very low calorie and sodium content may appear favourable, but the high free-sugar concentration classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, this product is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar load, which causes rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes, independently raises blood pressure risk (AHA 2018), and elevates triglycerides. Despite ginger's known anti-inflammatory gingerol content, the high sugar content negates any therapeutic benefit for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. The product is commonly consumed at breakfast in Singapore and is available across supermarkets, convenience stores, and coffee shops. It is generally considered halal and vegetarian but is not vegan due to potential milk derivatives in the non-dairy creamer. Individuals seeking the health benefits of ginger should opt for freshly brewed unsweetened ginger tea instead.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink instant ginger tea?
How many calories in a sachet of 3-in-1 ginger tea?
Is instant ginger tea good for blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of 3-in-1 ginger tea?
Is 3-in-1 ginger tea suitable for weight loss?
Can I drink ginger tea if I have hypertension?
What are the ingredients in 3-in-1 ginger tea?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09020001) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from an analogous sucrose-dominant beverage reference, which is the best available approved-source evidence for this product type.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database (D09020001); this ultra-processed instant beverage is formulated without added fat, and the non-dairy creamer component contributes negligible fat at the reported dilution ratio, resulting in 0.0 g fat/100 g as explicitly reported by HPB. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same source. protein_g is 0.02 g/100 g (trace level, not zero). calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 by HPB; these are confirmed zero per the approved source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown data for this instant ginger tea premix are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (D09020001) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on sucrose-dominant sugar profile per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving calculated on 20 g sachet basis: (65 × 10.31 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 20 g / 100) / 100 = 1.34 per sachet. Dietary tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from input are retained as contextually accurate (sodium 4 mg/100 g; energy 41 kcal/100 g) but do not override the high-sugar SSB classification for clinical purposes.
